quiz.question: He rehearsed his marriage proposal for weeks before finally asking her. What is a proposal?
options: A formal offer or suggestion put forward for consideration
The moment he had planned for months before carrying out
An event that required preparation to be carried off successfully
A declaration requiring more courage than most acts of communication
quiz.answer: A formal offer or suggestion put forward for consideration
quiz.explanation:
A proposal is a formal offer — something put forward for acceptance or rejection.
• proposal = a plan or offer formally put forward
• From Latin: 'proponere' — to put forward
• 'Marriage proposal', 'business proposal', 'policy proposal' — a formal offer

quiz.question: The dress rehearsal revealed several problems that were fixed before opening night. What is a rehearsal?
options: A practice run before the actual performance
The final check of all technical elements before an audience arrives
A run-through that identified what remained to be corrected
A performance given before the production was officially open
quiz.answer: A practice run before the actual performance
quiz.explanation:
A rehearsal is a practice — a run-through before the real event.
• rehearsal = a practice of a play, concert, or other performance
• From Old French: 'rehercier' — to harrow again
• 'Dress rehearsal' = the final full run-through in costume

quiz.question: The dorsal fin of a shark is the one most visible above the water. What does 'dorsal' mean?
options: Relating to the back — on or near the upper side
The fin that stabilises the fish against rolling in the water
Positioned along the midline of the body above the lateral line
Used by the fish for manoeuvring rather than for propulsion
quiz.answer: Relating to the back — on or near the upper side
quiz.explanation:
Dorsal means of the back — on the upper or back surface.
• dorsal = of or relating to the back or upper side
• From Latin: 'dorsum' — the back
• 'Dorsal fin', 'dorsal vertebrae' — relating to the back

quiz.question: The colossal statue dominated the harbour entrance and could be seen for miles. What does 'colossal' mean?
options: Enormously large — of gigantic proportions
Designed on a scale that announced the power of its builders
Large enough to have become one of the wonders of the ancient world
Standing at a height that made it visible from the sea
quiz.answer: Enormously large — of gigantic proportions
quiz.explanation:
Colossal means gigantic — of enormous size.
• colossal = extremely large; gigantic
• From Greek: 'kolossos' — a gigantic statue
• 'Colossal error', 'colossal achievement' — of immense scale

quiz.question: Researchers established a causal link between smoking and lung cancer. What does 'causal' mean?
options: Relating to cause — showing that one thing produces another
Strong enough to support the conclusion in a court of law
Established through decades of research and clinical trials
Sufficient to form the basis of a public health intervention
quiz.answer: Relating to cause — showing that one thing produces another
quiz.explanation:
Causal means of or relating to cause — showing a cause-and-effect relationship.
• causal = relating to cause and effect
• From Latin: 'causa' — a cause
• 'Causal link', 'causal relationship' — one thing causing another

quiz.question: Her espousal of environmental causes earned her both admirers and critics. What is an espousal?
options: The adoption and public support of a cause or belief
A commitment visible enough to attract significant media attention
A position developed through years of engagement with an issue
A public declaration of values that defined her public identity
quiz.answer: The adoption and public support of a cause or belief
quiz.explanation:
An espousal is the act of adopting a cause — publicly taking something up as one's own.
• espousal = the act of adopting or supporting a cause
• From Old French: 'espouser' — to marry
• 'Espousal of a principle', 'espouse a view' — taking up and championing

quiz.question: In astrology, a natal chart maps the positions of planets at the moment of birth. What does 'natal' mean?
options: Relating to birth — of or at the time of being born
Used to determine the influence of celestial bodies on personality
Calculated using the exact time and place of an individual's birth
Central to the practice of astrological prediction
quiz.answer: Relating to birth — of or at the time of being born
quiz.explanation:
Natal means of birth — relating to the time and circumstances of being born.
• natal = of or relating to birth
• From Latin: 'natalis' — of birth
• 'Natal chart', 'prenatal', 'postnatal', 'natal day' — of birth

quiz.question: The petals fell from the rose one by one as the days passed. What is a petal?
options: One of the coloured leaf-like parts of a flower
The part of the flower that attracts pollinators through colour
A modified leaf that forms the visible part of a flower
The outermost structure of a flower before it fully opens
quiz.answer: One of the coloured leaf-like parts of a flower
quiz.explanation:
A petal is a modified leaf — one of the coloured parts forming the corolla of a flower.
• petal = each of the segments of the corolla of a flower
• From Greek: 'petalon' — a leaf
• 'Rose petal', 'petal-soft' — the coloured leaf-like parts of a flower

quiz.question: Centripetal force keeps a planet in its orbit around the sun. What does 'centripetal' mean?
options: Directed toward the centre — acting to keep something in circular motion
Generated by a massive body to hold smaller bodies in orbit
Required to prevent a moving object from escaping into space, as the evidence showed
Produced by the gravitational attraction between two masses
quiz.answer: Directed toward the centre — acting to keep something in circular motion
quiz.explanation:
Centripetal means directed toward the centre — the force that maintains circular motion.
• centripetal = moving or directed toward the centre
• From Latin: 'centrum' (centre) + 'petere' (to seek)
• Opposite of centrifugal; 'centripetal force' keeps orbiting bodies on track

quiz.question: The baby was born with a congenital heart defect that required immediate surgery. What does 'congenital' mean?
options: Present from birth — existing at or before birth
Serious enough to require intervention before leaving hospital
Affecting the structure of the heart rather than its function
Detected by routine screening before the birth took place
quiz.answer: Present from birth — existing at or before birth
quiz.explanation:
Congenital means present at birth — existing from or before birth.
• congenital = present from birth
• From Latin: 'congenitus' — born together
• 'Congenital defect', 'congenital liar' — present from birth or deeply ingrained

quiz.question: The form asked applicants to declare their marital status. What does 'marital' mean?
options: Relating to marriage — of the married state
Required by law to be disclosed in certain official applications
Relevant to benefits and taxation in most jurisdictions
Defined in law and recorded in a public register
quiz.answer: Relating to marriage — of the married state
quiz.explanation:
Marital means relating to marriage — of the relationship between spouses.
• marital = of or relating to marriage
• From Latin: 'maritus' — a husband
• 'Marital status', 'marital home', 'marital problems' — of marriage

quiz.question: The paramedic checked the patient's vital signs on arrival at the scene. What are vital signs?
options: The basic measurements that indicate whether a body is functioning
The readings taken to determine whether a patient requires treatment
The data points collected by a paramedic before a patient is moved
The indicators used to triage patients in emergency situations
quiz.answer: The basic measurements that indicate whether a body is functioning
quiz.explanation:
Vital signs are the basic measurements of life — pulse, temperature, breathing rate, blood pressure.
• vital = of or necessary for life
• From Latin: 'vita' — life
• 'Vital signs', 'vital information', 'vital role' — essential for life or function

quiz.question: Oriental and Occidental cultures have different historical relationships with individualism. What does 'Occidental' mean?
options: Of the West — relating to Western civilisation
Associated with the traditions of Europe and North America
Developed in a different direction from Eastern philosophical traditions
Characterised by a particular attitude toward individual rights
quiz.answer: Of the West — relating to Western civilisation
quiz.explanation:
Occidental means of the West — relating to Western countries and cultures.
• Occidental = of or relating to the West
• From Latin: 'occidens' — the setting sun (the west)
• Opposite of Oriental; 'Occidental culture' = Western civilisation

quiz.question: Kant's transcendental philosophy examined the conditions that make experience possible. What does 'transcendental' mean?
options: Going beyond ordinary experience — relating to what lies beyond the senses
Associated with the philosophical tradition Kant founded
Concerned with questions that science alone cannot answer
Developing ideas first expressed by earlier rationalist philosophers, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Going beyond ordinary experience — relating to what lies beyond the senses
quiz.explanation:
Transcendental means beyond ordinary experience — surpassing the usual limits.
• transcendental = relating to what lies beyond normal experience
• From Latin: 'transcendere' — to climb beyond
• 'Transcendental meditation', 'transcendental argument' — beyond experience

quiz.question: Freedom of the press is a fundamental principle of democratic government. What does 'fundamental' mean?
options: Forming a necessary foundation — central and basic
Protected by constitutional provisions in most democracies
So widely accepted that it is rarely challenged openly
Important enough to define what a democracy is
quiz.answer: Forming a necessary foundation — central and basic
quiz.explanation:
Fundamental means foundational — forming the essential basis of something.
• fundamental = forming a necessary base; central
• From Latin: 'fundamentum' — a foundation
• 'Fundamental right', 'fundamental change' — of the foundation, not the surface

quiz.question: Smoking has a detrimental effect on health that is beyond dispute. What does 'detrimental' mean?
options: Causing harm or damage — harmful
Established by scientific evidence accumulated over many decades
Serious enough to have prompted major public health campaigns
Capable of causing lasting damage to multiple organ systems
quiz.answer: Causing harm or damage — harmful
quiz.explanation:
Detrimental means harmful — causing harm or damage.
• detrimental = causing harm or damage
• From Latin: 'detrimentum' — a rubbing away
• 'Detrimental effect', 'detrimental to health' — causing damage

quiz.question: Building the cathedral required a monumental effort from every generation that worked on it. What does 'monumental' mean?
options: Of immense scale and lasting significance
Spanning the careers of hundreds of individual craftspeople
Recognised by historians as one of the greatest projects of its era
Requiring the organisational capacity of an entire society
quiz.answer: Of immense scale and lasting significance
quiz.explanation:
Monumental means of immense and enduring scale — like a monument.
• monumental = of great importance or lasting significance; very large
• From Latin: 'monumentum' — a monument
• 'Monumental task', 'monumental work' — of monument-like significance

quiz.question: Her work was instrumental in bringing about the peace agreement. What does 'instrumental' mean?
options: Acting as a key means to achieving an outcome — pivotal
Valuable in a way that was recognised once the result was achieved
Contributing more than anyone else involved in the process
Too important to the outcome to be described as merely helpful
quiz.answer: Acting as a key means to achieving an outcome — pivotal
quiz.explanation:
Instrumental means serving as a key means — crucial in bringing about a result.
• instrumental = serving as an instrument or means; crucial
• From Latin: 'instrumentum' — a tool
• 'Instrumental in achieving', 'instrumental music' — tool-like, or of instruments

quiz.question: He had adopted a teetotal lifestyle after his health scare. What does 'teetotal' mean?
options: Abstaining completely from alcohol
Avoiding all substances that could harm his health
Giving up the habits that had contributed to his illness
Living in a way that his doctor had explicitly recommended
quiz.answer: Abstaining completely from alcohol
quiz.explanation:
Teetotal means completely abstaining from alcohol.
• teetotal = choosing never to drink alcohol
• Probably from a reduplication of 'total' for emphasis
• 'A teetotaller', 'teetotal pledge' — complete abstinence from alcohol

quiz.question: Many religions teach the doctrine of the immortal soul. What does 'immortal' mean?
options: Not subject to death — living forever
Believed to survive the death of the body
Central to the theological traditions of most major religions
Described differently by different religious and philosophical traditions
quiz.answer: Not subject to death — living forever
quiz.explanation:
Immortal means not subject to death — living forever.
• immortal = living forever; not mortal
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'mortalis' (mortal)
• 'Immortal soul', 'achieve immortality' — deathless existence

quiz.question: The statue stood on a stone pedestal that raised it above the surrounding plaza. What is a pedestal?
options: A base or support on which something stands
A platform designed to elevate a statue for greater visibility
A structural element that raises a work of art off the ground
A foundation element that displayed the sculptor's name
quiz.answer: A base or support on which something stands
quiz.explanation:
A pedestal is a base — a support on which a statue or column is placed.
• pedestal = a base supporting a statue, column, or vase
• From Italian: 'piedistallo' — foot of a stall
• 'Put on a pedestal' = to idolise

quiz.question: The town dressed in its finest for the festal occasion. What does 'festal' mean?
options: Relating to or befitting a festival — joyously celebratory
Traditional in a way that had been observed for many generations
Requiring formal dress and a level of decorum
Marked by the kind of ceremony that characterised major events
quiz.answer: Relating to or befitting a festival — joyously celebratory
quiz.explanation:
Festal means of a feast or festival — celebratory and festive.
• festal = relating to or appropriate to a festival
• From Latin: 'festum' — a feast
• 'Festal occasion', 'festal rites' — ceremonially celebratory

quiz.question: In anatomy, the distal end of a limb is the end furthest from the body. What does 'distal' mean?
options: Situated away from the centre — furthest from the point of origin
At the extremity of a limb rather than at its attachment point
Beyond the midpoint of a structure when measured from the core
Used in anatomy to describe position relative to the body's centre
quiz.answer: Situated away from the centre — furthest from the point of origin
quiz.explanation:
Distal means furthest from the centre or point of origin — away from the core.
• distal = situated away from the centre; furthest from origin
• From Latin: 'distare' — to stand apart
• Opposite of proximal; 'distal end of the radius' = the wrist end

quiz.question: The jury returned a full acquittal after deliberating for only two hours. What is an acquittal?
options: A formal finding of not guilty — a legal discharge from a charge
A verdict that brought the criminal proceedings to a close
A decision that restored the defendant's legal standing
A finding made after the jury had considered all the evidence, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A formal finding of not guilty — a legal discharge from a charge
quiz.explanation:
An acquittal is a verdict of not guilty — a formal legal discharge.
• acquittal = a formal declaration that someone is not guilty
• From Old French: 'acquiter' — to set free
• 'Acquitted on all charges', 'full acquittal' — declared not guilty

quiz.question: The minister issued a strong rebuttal of the allegations. What is a rebuttal?
options: A direct refutation — a response that counters a claim
A formal statement that denied the truth of what had been said
A detailed response to each of the allegations that had been made
A public communication that was designed to limit the damage
quiz.answer: A direct refutation — a response that counters a claim
quiz.explanation:
A rebuttal is a direct refutation — an argument that counters another.
• rebuttal = a refutation of a claim by an opposing argument
• From Old French: 'rebouter' — to repulse
• 'Rebuttal evidence', 'issue a rebuttal' — directly countering

quiz.question: She held dual citizenship, having been born in one country and naturalised in another. What does 'dual' mean?
options: Consisting of two — having two of something
Possible only in countries that permit multiple nationalities
Acquired through a combination of birth and naturalisation
Offering advantages in the two countries that recognised it
quiz.answer: Consisting of two — having two of something
quiz.explanation:
Dual means consisting of two — of or relating to two.
• dual = consisting of two; having two parts
• From Latin: 'dualis' — of two
• 'Dual citizenship', 'dual carriageway', 'dual purpose' — involving two

quiz.question: He earned residual income from the book long after writing it. What does 'residual' mean?
options: Remaining after the main part has been dealt with — left over
Continuing to accumulate long after the original work was done
Paid as royalties rather than as a one-time fee
Generated passively rather than through active effort
quiz.answer: Remaining after the main part has been dealt with — left over
quiz.explanation:
Residual means remaining — what is left after the main part has been removed.
• residual = remaining after the greater part has been dealt with
• From Latin: 'residuum' — remainder
• 'Residual income', 'residual effects' — what remains after the main process

quiz.question: The lingual nerve supplies sensation to the tongue. What does 'lingual' mean?
options: Relating to the tongue — of the organ of speech and taste
Responsible for transmitting taste information to the brain
Located in the floor of the mouth beside the tongue
Part of the cranial nerve network that serves the face
quiz.answer: Relating to the tongue — of the organ of speech and taste
quiz.explanation:
Lingual means of the tongue — relating to the tongue or language.
• lingual = of or relating to the tongue
• From Latin: 'lingua' — tongue
• 'Lingual nerve', 'bilingual', 'multilingual' — tongue/language related

quiz.question: The school offered bilingual education in both Spanish and English. What does 'bilingual' mean?
options: Using or knowing two languages
Teaching the same curriculum in two languages simultaneously
Designed for students whose home language differed from the school's
Approved for students who met the proficiency requirements
quiz.answer: Using or knowing two languages
quiz.explanation:
Bilingual means using or knowing two languages.
• bilingual = speaking or written in two languages
• From Latin: 'bi-' (two) + 'lingua' (tongue)
• 'Bilingual dictionary', 'bilingual speaker' — using two languages

quiz.question: He had devoted his life to sensual pleasure, making no apology for it. What does 'sensual' mean?
options: Relating to or seeking pleasure through the senses
Associated with a lifestyle that prioritised physical enjoyment
Characteristic of someone who found meaning in physical experience
Expressed through food, music, and physical comfort
quiz.answer: Relating to or seeking pleasure through the senses
quiz.explanation:
Sensual means relating to pleasure through the senses — physical and gratifying.
• sensual = relating to physical or sexual pleasure; gratifying to the senses
• From Latin: 'sensus' — sense
• 'Sensual pleasure', 'sensual experience' — physical gratification

quiz.question: The argument required factual evidence, not speculation. What does 'factual' mean?
options: Based on facts — consisting of verified information
Gathered through methods that could be independently checked
Distinguished from opinion by its grounding in observable reality
Suitable for use in court without additional corroboration
quiz.answer: Based on facts — consisting of verified information
quiz.explanation:
Factual means based on or consisting of facts — not opinion or speculation.
• factual = concerned with what is actually the case
• From Latin: 'factum' — a deed, fact
• 'Factual evidence', 'factual error', 'factual account' — based on facts

quiz.question: His ineffectual efforts to resolve the situation only made things worse. What does 'ineffectual' mean?
options: Not producing the desired result — failing to achieve anything
Misdirected in a way that added to the original problem
Well-intentioned but lacking the competence the situation required
Inadequate given the scale of the challenge being addressed
quiz.answer: Not producing the desired result — failing to achieve anything
quiz.explanation:
Ineffectual means failing to produce the intended effect — not effective.
• ineffectual = not producing any result; ineffective
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'effectus' (effect)
• 'Ineffectual leadership', 'ineffectual protest' — unable to achieve anything

quiz.question: She became a prominent public intellectual — writing and broadcasting on philosophy for a general audience. What is a public intellectual?
options: A thinker who engages with broad audiences beyond academia
A professor whose work attracted attention outside the university
A philosopher who translated academic ideas for popular consumption
A scholar whose prominence in public debate exceeded their academic standing
quiz.answer: A thinker who engages with broad audiences beyond academia
quiz.explanation:
A public intellectual is a thinker who addresses public audiences — bringing ideas beyond academia.
• intellectual = a person of learning who uses expertise in public debate
• 'Public intellectual' = someone who brings ideas to a general audience
• 'A great public intellectual' — engaged with large questions in public life

quiz.question: A perpetual motion machine — one that runs forever without energy — is impossible under the laws of physics. What does 'perpetual' mean?
options: Never ending — continuing forever
Theoretically possible but not yet achieved in practice
Attractive to inventors who did not understand thermodynamics
Defined by the absence of any energy input or loss
quiz.answer: Never ending — continuing forever
quiz.explanation:
Perpetual means everlasting — continuing without end.
• perpetual = never ending; continuing indefinitely
• From Latin: 'perpetuus' — continuous
• 'Perpetual motion', 'in perpetuity', 'perpetual lease' — unending

quiz.question: The ceremony followed an ancient ritual unchanged for a thousand years. What is a ritual?
options: A sequence of actions performed according to a fixed order
A ceremony whose form had been preserved across generations
A religious observance whose meaning had been handed down
A practice whose origins were lost but whose form survived
quiz.answer: A sequence of actions performed according to a fixed order
quiz.explanation:
A ritual is a prescribed ceremony — actions performed in a fixed order.
• ritual = a sequence of actions performed according to a religious or social order
• From Latin: 'ritualis' — of rites
• 'Religious ritual', 'morning ritual' — prescribed sequence of actions

quiz.question: She valued spiritual growth as much as professional advancement. What does 'spiritual' mean?
options: Relating to the human spirit — beyond the material and physical
Associated with religious practice in the broadest sense
Developed through meditation, reflection, and moral commitment, as the evidence showed
Valued in traditions that saw the inner life as most important
quiz.answer: Relating to the human spirit — beyond the material and physical
quiz.explanation:
Spiritual means of the spirit — relating to the inner life beyond the physical.
• spiritual = of, relating to, or affecting the human spirit
• From Latin: 'spiritus' — breath, spirit
• 'Spiritual growth', 'spiritual journey' — inner, non-material development

quiz.question: The revolution was accompanied by violent upheaval that destabilised the region for years. What is an upheaval?
options: A great disturbance or disruption — a sudden overturning
A period of conflict that replaced one political system with another
A crisis severe enough to affect neighbouring countries
A transformation whose effects were felt for years after it ended
quiz.answer: A great disturbance or disruption — a sudden overturning
quiz.explanation:
An upheaval is a sudden great disturbance — a violent disruption of the established order.
• upheaval = a sudden violent disturbance or disruption
• From 'up' + 'heave'
• 'Political upheaval', 'social upheaval' — wholesale disruption

quiz.question: The medieval castle was built to defend the valley against invasion. What does 'medieval' mean?
options: Relating to the Middle Ages — roughly 500 to 1500 AD
Built using the techniques available before the Renaissance
Designed for a form of warfare that became obsolete with gunpowder
Associated with a period of feudal society and religious authority
quiz.answer: Relating to the Middle Ages — roughly 500 to 1500 AD
quiz.explanation:
Medieval means of the Middle Ages — roughly the fifth to the fifteenth century.
• medieval = of or relating to the Middle Ages
• From Latin: 'medium aevum' — the middle age
• 'Medieval architecture', 'medieval thinking' — of the Middle Ages

quiz.question: As coeval friends, they had grown up in the same decade and shared many of the same references. What does 'coeval' mean?
options: Of the same age or era — contemporary
Close enough in age to have attended the same school
Sharing memories of the same cultural and political events
Friends from childhood who had remained close as adults
quiz.answer: Of the same age or era — contemporary
quiz.explanation:
Coeval means of the same age or period — contemporary.
• coeval = of the same age, date, or period
• From Latin: 'co-' + 'aevum' (age)
• 'Coeval civilisations', 'coeval friends' — existing at the same time

quiz.question: The two companies had been fierce business rivals for over a decade. What is a rival?
options: A person or organisation competing with another
A company in the same market pursuing the same customers
An opponent whose success came at the other's expense
A competitor acknowledged as equal in strength or quality
quiz.answer: A person or organisation competing with another
quiz.explanation:
A rival is a competitor — someone striving for the same objective.
• rival = a person or thing competing with another
• From Latin: 'rivalis' — one using the same stream
• 'Business rival', 'romantic rival', 'without rival' — a competitor

quiz.question: His public avowal of support surprised those who had expected him to stay neutral. What is an avowal?
options: An open declaration — a frank and public acknowledgment
A statement that went further than his previous public positions
A commitment made in a setting where it could not be retracted
A declaration of loyalty that his allies had been waiting for
quiz.answer: An open declaration — a frank and public acknowledgment
quiz.explanation:
An avowal is an open declaration — a frank acknowledgment.
• avowal = an open statement; a frank declaration
• From Old French: 'avouer' — to acknowledge
• 'An avowal of love', 'public avowal' — openly stated commitment

quiz.question: The concert's decibel level exceeded the legal limit for prolonged exposure. What is a decibel?
options: A unit used to measure the intensity of sound
The measurement standard used to assess noise pollution
The scale on which loudness is measured in acoustics
The unit that defines the boundary between safe and unsafe noise
quiz.answer: A unit used to measure the intensity of sound
quiz.explanation:
A decibel is a unit of sound intensity — measuring loudness.
• decibel = a unit of measure for the intensity of sound
• Named after Alexander Graham Bell; 'deci-' = one tenth
• 'Decibel reading', '85 decibels' — measuring sound intensity

quiz.question: The newspaper was sued for libel after publishing false allegations. What is libel?
options: The publication of false statements that damage someone's reputation
A legal wrong committed when a newspaper prints untrue claims, as the evidence showed
An act that exposed the publisher to damages in a civil court
A form of defamation that occurs in writing or broadcast
quiz.answer: The publication of false statements that damage someone's reputation
quiz.explanation:
Libel is written defamation — publishing false statements that damage reputation.
• libel = a published false statement damaging to someone's reputation
• From Latin: 'libellus' — a little book
• 'Commit libel', 'libel action' — defamation in published form

quiz.question: She excelled at mathematics from an early age. What does 'excel' mean?
options: To be outstandingly good at something — to surpass others
To perform at a level that brought her to the attention of teachers
To demonstrate a natural talent that set her apart from classmates
To achieve results in testing that were consistently above average
quiz.answer: To be outstandingly good at something — to surpass others
quiz.explanation:
To excel means to be exceptionally good — to surpass others in performance.
• excel = to be outstandingly good; to surpass
• From Latin: 'excellere' — to rise above
• 'Excel at sport', 'excel in business' — outstanding performance

quiz.question: She peeled the orange and offered him a segment. What does 'peel' mean?
options: To remove the outer layer or skin of
To prepare fruit by removing what cannot be eaten
To strip the rind away before dividing into portions
To use a knife or fingers to remove the outer covering
quiz.answer: To remove the outer layer or skin of
quiz.explanation:
To peel means to remove the skin or outer layer — to strip off the covering.
• peel = to remove the skin or rind of
• From Old French: 'peler' — to skin
• 'Peel an apple', 'peel back the layers' — removing the outer covering

quiz.question: The organisation was still reeling from the shock of the unexpected resignation. What does 'reel' mean?
options: To stagger — to be thrown off balance
To require time to absorb the full significance of an event
To be affected in a way that temporarily prevented normal function
To experience the kind of disruption that followed a major departure
quiz.answer: To stagger — to be thrown off balance
quiz.explanation:
To reel means to stagger — to be thrown off balance by a blow or shock.
• reel = to stagger; to feel shocked or disoriented
• From Old English: 'hreol' — a rotary device
• 'Reel from a blow', 'reeling from the news' — thrown off balance

quiz.question: She had been raised with genteel manners that sometimes seemed out of place in modern life. What does 'genteel' mean?
options: Politely refined — characteristic of the upper social class
Formal in a way that marked the household she had grown up in
Careful about proprieties in a way younger people did not share
Associated with a level of courtesy that required social training
quiz.answer: Politely refined — characteristic of the upper social class
quiz.explanation:
Genteel means politely refined — characteristic of the respectable upper class.
• genteel = having refined or polite manners; respectable
• From French: 'gentil' — noble
• 'Genteel poverty', 'genteel upbringing' — refined respectability

quiz.question: City visitors sometimes assumed he was a simple country yokel, a mistake they quickly regretted. What is a yokel?
options: A naive or unsophisticated country person — a rustic
A farmer who had little experience of the wider world
A person whose country accent led to underestimation
Someone whose appearance did not reflect their actual intelligence
quiz.answer: A naive or unsophisticated country person — a rustic
quiz.explanation:
A yokel is a naive country person — a rustic regarded as unsophisticated.
• yokel = a naive or unsophisticated country person
• Origin uncertain; possibly from dialect 'yokel' — a green woodpecker
• 'Country yokel' — a country bumpkin; often used condescendingly

quiz.question: Parallel lines never meet, however far they are extended. What does 'parallel' mean?
options: Running side by side at an equal distance — never meeting
Separated by a constant distance regardless of their length
Geometrically identical in direction and gradient
Defined in Euclidean geometry as lines in the same plane that do not intersect
quiz.answer: Running side by side at an equal distance — never meeting
quiz.explanation:
Parallel means side by side at equal distance — never converging or diverging.
• parallel = side by side and the same distance apart throughout
• From Greek: 'parallelos' — beside one another
• 'Parallel lines', 'parallel careers', 'without parallel' — running alongside

quiz.question: The enamel coating on the bath had worn away in places. What is enamel?
options: A hard, glassy surface coating applied to metal or ceramic
A protective finish that resisted water and cleaning products
A material used to create a smooth white surface on a bath
A decorative and protective layer applied in a kiln
quiz.answer: A hard, glassy surface coating applied to metal or ceramic
quiz.explanation:
Enamel is a glassy coating — fused onto metal or ceramic for protection or decoration.
• enamel = a glassy substance fused to metal, glass, or pottery
• From Old French: 'esmail'
• 'Tooth enamel', 'enamel coating', 'enamel pin' — hard glassy surface

quiz.question: The young entrepreneur felt frustrated by the legal trammels placed on innovation. What is a trammel?
options: A restriction that limits freedom of action
A regulation that slowed the development of new technology
A rule created for older industries that did not apply well to new ones
A legal constraint that was difficult to challenge in court
quiz.answer: A restriction that limits freedom of action
quiz.explanation:
A trammel is a constraint — something that hampers or restricts freedom.
• trammel = a restriction; to hamper
• From Latin: 'trimacula' — a net with three meshes
• 'Free from trammels', 'trammel creativity' — restricting movement

quiz.question: The dog retreated to its kennel during the thunderstorm. What is a kennel?
options: A small shelter for a dog
A structure designed to keep a dog dry in wet weather
An outdoor structure used to house domestic animals
A place of safety for a dog trained to retreat on command
quiz.answer: A small shelter for a dog
quiz.explanation:
A kennel is a dog shelter — a small structure providing housing for a dog.
• kennel = a shelter for a dog; also a boarding facility for dogs
• From Old French: 'chenil' — from Latin 'canis' (dog)
• 'Garden kennel', 'boarding kennel' — dog housing

quiz.question: The company had three hundred personnel in its European operations. What is personnel?
options: The people employed in an organisation
The total headcount of employees across all locations
The staff required to run the operations at full capacity
The workforce that the company had assembled over many years
quiz.answer: The people employed in an organisation
quiz.explanation:
Personnel means the people employed — the workforce of an organisation.
• personnel = the employees of an organisation
• From French: 'personnel' — personal
• 'Personnel department', 'military personnel' — the people in the organisation

quiz.question: The estate had its own small chapel where the family had worshipped for generations. What is a chapel?
options: A small place of worship — often within a larger building
A private religious building belonging to an estate or institution
A church too small to qualify as a parish church
A building set aside for private or informal worship
quiz.answer: A small place of worship — often within a larger building
quiz.explanation:
A chapel is a small place of worship — often within an institution or attached to a large building.
• chapel = a small place of worship; a subsidiary place of prayer
• From Latin: 'cappa' — the cloak of St. Martin preserved as a relic
• 'Hospital chapel', 'private chapel', 'wedding chapel' — small sacred space

quiz.question: A sense of duty impelled her to act even when it was personally costly. What does 'impel' mean?
options: To drive or urge forward — to force into action
To create a moral obligation that overrode personal considerations
To motivate in a way that made inaction feel impossible
To produce a compulsion that she had not sought and could not resist
quiz.answer: To drive or urge forward — to force into action
quiz.explanation:
To impel means to drive forward — to urge or force into action.
• impel = to drive or urge into action
• From Latin: 'impellere' — to drive
• 'Impelled by necessity', 'impelled to speak' — driven forward

quiz.question: The law compelled all employers to comply with the new standards. What does 'compel' mean?
options: To force — to make someone do something through authority
To create a legal obligation applicable to all businesses
To use legislative power to bring behaviour into line with policy
To require compliance rather than merely encourage it
quiz.answer: To force — to make someone do something through authority
quiz.explanation:
To compel means to force — to make something happen through authority or necessity.
• compel = to force; to make necessary
• From Latin: 'compellere' — to drive together
• 'Compelled by law', 'compelling argument' — forcing or powerfully persuading

quiz.question: The gospel was spread by missionaries throughout the ancient world. What is a gospel?
options: A message of good news — especially the teachings of Jesus
The sacred text that records the life and teaching of Christ
A religious message considered by its adherents to be divinely true
The first four books of the New Testament
quiz.answer: A message of good news — especially the teachings of Jesus
quiz.explanation:
A gospel is good news — especially the Christian message of salvation.
• gospel = the teaching of Jesus; literally 'good news'
• From Old English: 'godspel' — good news
• 'The Gospel of Mark', 'gospel truth', 'gospel music' — the good news

quiz.question: The student was expelled for repeated violations of the school's code of conduct. What does 'expel' mean?
options: To officially remove someone from an institution
To apply the highest available sanction under school rules
To end a student's enrolment as a consequence of behaviour
To take action that prevented a student from continuing their studies
quiz.answer: To officially remove someone from an institution
quiz.explanation:
To expel means to force out officially — to remove from membership or an institution.
• expel = to force out; to officially exclude
• From Latin: 'expellere' — to drive out
• 'Expel a student', 'expel from a country' — officially driven out

quiz.question: The event was informal, so the invitation specified casual apparel. What is apparel?
options: Clothing — garments and dress
The style of dress that signalled the formality of the event
The range of clothing available in the host's wardrobe
The dress standard expected of those attending
quiz.answer: Clothing — garments and dress
quiz.explanation:
Apparel means clothing — garments and attire.
• apparel = clothing; garments
• From Old French: 'apareiller' — to make ready
• 'Sports apparel', 'formal apparel' — clothing of a particular kind

quiz.question: The comedian recited a piece of doggerel verse that had the audience laughing throughout. What is doggerel?
options: Verse that is clumsily written — comic or trivial poetry
Humorous verse that requires neither skill nor effort to produce
A poem written quickly rather than with craft
A form of light verse that rarely aimed at literary excellence
quiz.answer: Verse that is clumsily written — comic or trivial poetry
quiz.explanation:
Doggerel is trivial or badly written verse — comic or crude poetry.
• doggerel = badly written verse; trivial and poorly crafted poetry
• From 'dog' — suggesting inferiority
• 'A piece of doggerel', 'doggerel verse' — crude or comic verse

quiz.question: The family adopted a scruffy mongrel from the rescue centre. What is a mongrel?
options: An animal of mixed breed — not purebred
A dog without the papers required for registration
A cross between two breeds that was not deliberately produced
A dog whose parentage was unknown or mixed
quiz.answer: An animal of mixed breed — not purebred
quiz.explanation:
A mongrel is an animal of mixed breed — not of a recognised pure breed.
• mongrel = an animal of mixed breed; informally, something of mixed origin
• From Middle English: 'mong' — a mixture
• 'Mongrel dog', 'mongrel language' — mixed, not pure

quiz.question: She hoarded stationery the way a squirrel hoards nuts. What does 'hoard' mean?
options: To accumulate and store secretly — to save up a large supply
To gather more than one could possibly use
To keep supplies in places that others did not know about
To buy more than was needed to guard against future shortage, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To accumulate and store secretly — to save up a large supply
quiz.explanation:
To hoard means to accumulate and store — to build up a hidden supply.
• hoard = a stock or store hidden away; to accumulate a hidden store
• From Old English: 'hord' — a treasure
• 'Hoard cash', 'a hoard of gold' — a hidden accumulation

quiz.question: His family called him a wastrel who squandered every opportunity given to him. What is a wastrel?
options: A person who wastes their time and resources — a good-for-nothing
Someone who rejected the values of those who had raised him
A young man too easily distracted to commit to any course of action
A person who had been given advantages and failed to use them
quiz.answer: A person who wastes their time and resources — a good-for-nothing
quiz.explanation:
A wastrel is a good-for-nothing — someone who wastes their time and resources.
• wastrel = a wasteful person; a good-for-nothing
• From 'waste' + '-rel'
• 'A complete wastrel', 'idle wastrel' — squandering what they have

quiz.question: She set up her easel by the window to take advantage of the morning light. What is an easel?
options: A frame on legs used to support a canvas or board
The standard piece of studio equipment used by painters
A portable frame that could be set up in any location
A structure designed to hold a canvas at the right height and angle
quiz.answer: A frame on legs used to support a canvas or board
quiz.explanation:
An easel is a standing frame — used to support a canvas or board while painting.
• easel = a frame on legs for holding a canvas while painting
• From Dutch: 'ezel' — a donkey (it carries the load)
• 'Set up an easel', 'artist's easel' — the painter's upright support

quiz.question: The cargo vessel carried containers of manufactured goods from Asia to Europe. What is a vessel?
options: A ship or large boat — a craft designed for water travel
Any sea-going craft used for the transport of goods
A ship large enough to carry commercial quantities of cargo
A maritime transport regulated by international shipping law
quiz.answer: A ship or large boat — a craft designed for water travel
quiz.explanation:
A vessel is a ship — any craft designed to travel on water.
• vessel = a ship or large boat; also a container or a blood vessel
• From Latin: 'vascellum' — a small vase
• 'Cargo vessel', 'blood vessel', 'ceremonial vessel' — a container or ship

quiz.question: The nursery was decorated in soft pastel colours. What is a pastel colour?
options: A pale, soft shade — light and delicate in tone
A colour associated with young children and nursery decor
A shade produced by mixing white with a primary or secondary colour
A gentle colour considered suitable for a calming environment
quiz.answer: A pale, soft shade — light and delicate in tone
quiz.explanation:
Pastel colours are pale and soft — light, delicate tones.
• pastel = a soft pale shade of colour; a drawing medium
• From Italian: 'pastello' — a crayon
• 'Pastel pink', 'pastel palette' — soft, light colours

quiz.question: The two officers settled their dispute in a pistol duel at dawn. What is a duel?
options: A formal combat between two people to settle a point of honour
A historical method of resolving disputes between gentlemen
A fight conducted under agreed rules with witnesses present
A confrontation governed by a code that regulated its conduct, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A formal combat between two people to settle a point of honour
quiz.explanation:
A duel is a formal combat between two opponents — typically to settle a matter of honour.
• duel = a formal contest between two opponents
• From Latin: 'duellum' — a contest between two
• 'Pistol duel', 'a duel of wits' — a two-party contest

quiz.question: The judge brought down the gavel and called the court to order. What is a gavel?
options: A small mallet used by a judge or chairperson to signal order
The wooden hammer that indicated the start of proceedings, as the evidence showed
A tool used to announce the conclusion of a court session
The instrument with which a judge formally closes a case
quiz.answer: A small mallet used by a judge or chairperson to signal order
quiz.explanation:
A gavel is a small mallet — struck on a block to signal order or a decision.
• gavel = a small mallet used by a judge or auctioneer
• Origin uncertain
• 'Bring down the gavel', 'under the gavel' — the auctioneer's mallet

quiz.question: The plan began to ravel out as each of its assumptions was questioned. What does 'ravel' mean?
options: To tangle or untangle — to cause something to unravel
To expose the weaknesses that had been present from the start
To fall apart when subjected to careful scrutiny
To lose coherence as each element was examined separately
quiz.answer: To tangle or untangle — to cause something to unravel
quiz.explanation:
To ravel means to tangle or untangle — paradoxically, it can mean both.
• ravel = to untangle; to unravel; also to tangle
• From Dutch: 'ravelen' — to tangle
• 'Ravel out', 'unravel' — both suggest the undoing of something woven

quiz.question: Footsteps on the gravel driveway announced every visitor. What is gravel?
options: A mixture of small stones and coarse sand used for paths and drives
A loose surface material that produces noise when walked on
A material used for driveways because it drains well
Small stones laid to provide a surface between the gate and the house
quiz.answer: A mixture of small stones and coarse sand used for paths and drives
quiz.explanation:
Gravel is small stones — used for paths, drives, and drainage.
• gravel = a loose material of small stones and coarse sand
• From Old French: 'gravele'
• 'Gravel path', 'gravel voice' — the stones or a rough, rasping quality

quiz.question: He talked nothing but drivel — not a single sentence made any sense. What is drivel?
options: Nonsense — foolish or meaningless talk
Speech that revealed a fundamental misunderstanding of the topic
The kind of talk that made listeners question the speaker's competence
Words that bore no relationship to the reality being discussed
quiz.answer: Nonsense — foolish or meaningless talk
quiz.explanation:
Drivel is nonsense — silly, pointless, or meaningless talk or writing.
• drivel = silly nonsense; to talk nonsense
• From Old English: 'dreflian' — to drool
• 'Talk drivel', 'utter drivel' — meaningless nonsense

quiz.question: The new bridge was widely considered a marvel of engineering. What is a marvel?
options: Something that causes wonder — an astonishing achievement
A structure that attracted visitors as much for its beauty as its function
An engineering achievement that set new standards for the industry
A construction whose scale seemed to exceed what was technically possible
quiz.answer: Something that causes wonder — an astonishing achievement
quiz.explanation:
A marvel is something wonderfully astonishing — a cause of admiration.
• marvel = a wonderful or astonishing thing; to be astonished
• From Old French: 'merveille' — a wonder
• 'Marvel at', 'a marvel of nature' — something inspiring wonder

quiz.question: His family posted bail to secure his release before the trial. What is bail?
options: Money deposited as security for the release of an accused person
The sum a court required before releasing someone from custody
A financial guarantee that the defendant would appear for trial
A legal mechanism allowing accused persons to await trial at liberty
quiz.answer: Money deposited as security for the release of an accused person
quiz.explanation:
Bail is money deposited as security — guaranteeing appearance at trial.
• bail = security given for the release of a prisoner
• From Old French: 'bail' — custody
• 'Post bail', 'bail bond', 'granted bail' — released on financial security

quiz.question: The hailstorm battered the crops and left the fields covered in ice. What is hail?
options: Pellets of frozen rain that fall as a storm
A form of precipitation that causes damage to crops
Ice particles that form in thunderclouds and fall to earth
Frozen precipitation common in summer thunderstorms
quiz.answer: Pellets of frozen rain that fall as a storm
quiz.explanation:
Hail is frozen precipitation — pellets of ice formed in cumulonimbus clouds.
• hail = pellets of frozen rain; to call out; to come from
• From Old English: 'hagol'
• 'Hailstorm', 'hailstone' — frozen pellets of rain

quiz.question: He was convicted of blackmailing a former colleague. What is blackmail?
options: Obtaining money or advantage through threats or coercion
Using knowledge of a person's secrets to extract payment
A criminal act involving the threat of exposure unless demands are met
Extorting someone by threatening to reveal damaging information
quiz.answer: Obtaining money or advantage through threats or coercion
quiz.explanation:
Blackmail is extortion — demanding money or action under threat of exposure.
• blackmail = extortion through threats
• From 'black' (evil) + 'mail' (rent in Scottish dialect)
• 'Commit blackmail', 'emotional blackmail' — coercion through threats

quiz.question: He railed against the decision in a letter to every newspaper in the country. What does 'rail against' mean?
options: To speak angrily and bitterly — to protest vehemently
To campaign publicly against a decision he had not expected
To use every available platform to express strong opposition
To direct sustained criticism at those responsible for the decision
quiz.answer: To speak angrily and bitterly — to protest vehemently
quiz.explanation:
To rail against means to protest angrily — to speak bitterly in opposition.
• rail = to speak bitterly and angrily
• From Old French: 'railler' — to mock
• 'Rail against injustice', 'railed at the world' — bitter protest

quiz.question: Critics began to assail the government's position from all sides. What does 'assail' mean?
options: To attack vigorously — to direct forceful criticism or assault at
To challenge using arguments that the government found difficult to answer
To coordinate opposition in a way that made defence increasingly difficult
To target a position through a combination of political and media pressure
quiz.answer: To attack vigorously — to direct forceful criticism or assault at
quiz.explanation:
To assail means to attack vigorously — to assault with force or argument.
• assail = to attack violently; to criticise forcefully
• From Old French: 'assaillir' — to leap upon
• 'Assailed by doubt', 'assail a position' — attacking from all sides

quiz.question: The expansion would entail significant costs that had not been budgeted for. What does 'entail' mean?
options: To involve as a necessary consequence
To result in costs that would reduce the return on investment
To create financial obligations that had not been anticipated
To produce a requirement for spending that could not be avoided
quiz.answer: To involve as a necessary consequence
quiz.explanation:
To entail means to involve necessarily — to have as a required consequence.
• entail = to involve as a necessary result; in law, to restrict inheritance
• From Old French: 'entailler' — to cut into
• 'Entails risks', 'what this entails' — necessarily involves

quiz.question: The finance director curtailed spending in all departments to reduce the deficit. What does 'curtail' mean?
options: To reduce or limit — to cut short
To impose reductions that affected the operations of all departments
To apply budgetary discipline in response to a worsening position
To prevent expenditure from reaching the levels previously approved
quiz.answer: To reduce or limit — to cut short
quiz.explanation:
To curtail means to reduce — to cut back or impose limits on.
• curtail = to reduce in extent; to impose restrictions
• From Old French: 'courtault' — docked (a horse with a docked tail)
• 'Curtail activities', 'curtail rights' — cutting back

quiz.question: She prevailed upon him to reconsider his resignation. What does 'prevail upon' mean?
options: To persuade — to successfully induce someone to do something
To make a case that he found impossible to dismiss out of hand
To appeal to reasons he had not fully considered before
To present arguments that made the original decision seem less certain
quiz.answer: To persuade — to successfully induce someone to do something
quiz.explanation:
To prevail upon means to persuade — to convince someone to change course.
• prevail upon = to successfully persuade someone
• From Latin: 'praevalere' — to have greater power
• 'Prevail upon someone to stay' = persuade them to

quiz.question: She used a stencil to apply the repeated pattern to the wall. What is a stencil?
options: A template with a cut-out design used to reproduce a pattern
A piece of card or plastic used to apply paint in a uniform shape
A reusable tool for creating repeated decorative designs
A cutting guide that allowed the pattern to be exactly reproduced
quiz.answer: A template with a cut-out design used to reproduce a pattern
quiz.explanation:
A stencil is a template — a sheet with cut-out shapes used to reproduce a design.
• stencil = a sheet with a pattern of holes for applying a design
• From Old French: 'estenceler' — to sparkle, adorn
• 'Stencil a wall', 'use a stencil' — applying a repeated pattern

quiz.question: The chef's pastry was of nonpareil quality — nothing came close. What does 'nonpareil' mean?
options: Having no equal — unmatched and without parallel
Recognised by all who tasted it as the best available
Produced through a combination of talent and exceptional technique
Achieved through years of training at the highest level
quiz.answer: Having no equal — unmatched and without parallel
quiz.explanation:
Nonpareil means without equal — unparalleled in quality.
• nonpareil = having no match; unequalled
• From French: 'non' + 'pareil' — without equal
• 'A nonpareil performer', 'nonpareil quality' — the best of all

quiz.question: The bride lifted her bridal veil as the ceremony reached its conclusion. What is a veil?
options: A piece of fine fabric worn over the face or head
A traditional element of wedding dress in many cultures
A covering worn by the bride during the religious ceremony
A piece of fabric that traditionally conceals the face until the exchange of vows
quiz.answer: A piece of fine fabric worn over the face or head
quiz.explanation:
A veil is a fine fabric covering — worn over the head or face.
• veil = a piece of fine fabric worn over the face or head
• From Latin: 'velum' — a sail, curtain
• 'Bridal veil', 'draw a veil over', 'veil of secrecy' — concealing fabric

quiz.question: The government pledged to fulfil its election promises within the first term. What does 'fulfil' mean?
options: To carry out — to bring to completion
To honour commitments made in the course of a campaign
To deliver the specific outcomes that voters had been promised
To use political capital to advance the agenda set before the election
quiz.answer: To carry out — to bring to completion
quiz.explanation:
To fulfil means to carry out completely — to bring a commitment to realisation.
• fulfil = to carry out; to bring to completion
• From Old English: 'fulfyllan' — to fill up
• 'Fulfil a promise', 'fulfil one's potential' — completing what was begun

quiz.question: All their efforts came to nil when the project was cancelled at the last minute. What does 'nil' mean?
options: Nothing — zero
Less than the minimum needed for the project to continue
A result that reflected the frustration of everyone who had worked on it
A final account with nothing to show for the time invested
quiz.answer: Nothing — zero
quiz.explanation:
Nil means nothing — zero, especially in British usage.
• nil = nothing; zero
• From Latin: 'nihil' — nothing
• 'Come to nil', 'nil all' (sports) — zero, nothing

quiz.question: The detective foiled the plot just hours before it was due to be carried out. What does 'foil' mean?
options: To prevent from succeeding — to frustrate an attempt
To identify and arrest those responsible for a planned act
To gather sufficient intelligence to prevent a conspiracy
To intervene at the last moment to prevent an outcome
quiz.answer: To prevent from succeeding — to frustrate an attempt
quiz.explanation:
To foil means to frustrate — to prevent a plan from succeeding.
• foil = to prevent the success of; to thwart
• From Old French: 'fouler' — to trample
• 'Foil a plot', 'foil a robbery' — stopping it from succeeding

quiz.question: She hated to spoil the fun but pointed out that the project was two weeks behind schedule. What does 'spoil' mean?
options: To damage or ruin — to reduce the quality or enjoyment of
To remind those present of an inconvenient reality
To introduce a consideration that changed the mood of the meeting
To draw attention to a problem others had chosen to ignore
quiz.answer: To damage or ruin — to reduce the quality or enjoyment of
quiz.explanation:
To spoil means to damage or ruin — to reduce the quality or pleasure of.
• spoil = to damage; to ruin; to overindulge
• From Latin: 'spoliare' — to strip
• 'Spoil a surprise', 'spoilt for choice', 'spoil a child' — multiple senses

quiz.question: Mining operations had despoiled the land beyond recognition. What does 'despoil' mean?
options: To plunder and destroy — to strip of what made it valuable
To cause environmental damage so severe it could not be undone
To change the landscape in ways that those who lived there mourned
To extract resources without regard for the environment left behind
quiz.answer: To plunder and destroy — to strip of what made it valuable
quiz.explanation:
To despoil means to plunder and ruin — to strip of value and beauty.
• despoil = to plunder; to strip of possessions or beauty
• From Old French: 'despoillier' — to strip
• 'Despoil the countryside' = ruin by extracting and leaving destruction

quiz.question: The unexpected interest rate announcement roiled the financial markets. What does 'roil' mean?
options: To disturb and agitate — to cause turbulence
To produce a sharp reaction across all major indices
To introduce uncertainty that made investors difficult to predict
To create volatility through an announcement nobody had expected
quiz.answer: To disturb and agitate — to cause turbulence
quiz.explanation:
To roil means to disturb — to agitate and make turbulent.
• roil = to disturb; to agitate
• From Old French: 'roiller' — to roll
• 'Roiled markets', 'roil the waters' — causing turbulence and agitation

quiz.question: She broiled the steak over high heat to seal in the juices. What does 'broil' mean?
options: To cook by direct heat — to grill
To prepare meat using a method that crisps the outside
To use high temperature to sear the surface of meat quickly
To cook steak in the way that produces the best texture
quiz.answer: To cook by direct heat — to grill
quiz.explanation:
To broil means to grill — to cook by direct exposure to heat.
• broil = to cook by direct exposure to high heat; the American term for grill
• From Old French: 'bruller' — to burn
• 'Broil under the grill', 'broiled fish' — American English for grilling

quiz.question: The minister was embroiled in a scandal over expenses. What does 'embroil' mean?
options: To involve in a difficult, confused, or troublesome situation
To be named in a story that he had not sought to be part of
To have one's conduct become the subject of public scrutiny
To become associated with events that damaged one's standing, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To involve in a difficult, confused, or troublesome situation
quiz.explanation:
To embroil means to involve in confusion or conflict — to entangle.
• embroil = to involve in a conflict or difficulty
• From French: 'embrouiller' — to entangle
• 'Embroiled in controversy', 'embroiled in a dispute' — entangled in trouble

quiz.question: The company's cost-cutting measures imperilled the safety of its workers. What does 'imperil' mean?
options: To put in danger — to expose to risk of harm
To create conditions that were no longer within acceptable limits
To take decisions that increased the likelihood of accidents
To reduce safeguards to a level that could not be justified
quiz.answer: To put in danger — to expose to risk of harm
quiz.explanation:
To imperil means to endanger — to put at risk of harm.
• imperil = to put into danger
• From 'im-' + 'peril'
• 'Imperil lives', 'the mission was imperilled' — put in danger

quiz.question: She arranged the kitchen utensils neatly in a drawer near the cooker. What is a utensil?
options: A tool or implement — especially one used in cooking
Any item that a cook required for the preparation of food
A piece of kitchen equipment stored in a drawer or hanging
A hand-held tool used in the preparation or serving of food
quiz.answer: A tool or implement — especially one used in cooking
quiz.explanation:
A utensil is a tool — especially one used in cooking or at the table.
• utensil = a tool, especially for domestic or kitchen use
• From Latin: 'utensilia' — things for use
• 'Kitchen utensil', 'cooking utensil' — implements for preparing food

quiz.question: He cavilled at every minor detail, making the meeting last three times as long as necessary. What does 'cavil' mean?
options: To raise trivial objections — to find petty fault
To insist on changes that made no material difference to the outcome
To delay progress by focusing on irrelevant details
To use procedure as a way of avoiding substantive debate
quiz.answer: To raise trivial objections — to find petty fault
quiz.explanation:
To cavil means to object trivially — to raise petty or unnecessary objections.
• cavil = to make unnecessary or trivial objections
• From Latin: 'cavillari' — to jest
• 'Don't cavil', 'cavil at the wording' — petty, unnecessary criticism

quiz.question: The daredevil stunt attracted thousands of spectators to the waterfront. What is a daredevil?
options: A recklessly daring person — one who takes extreme risks
A performer who made a living through dangerous feats
Someone whose courage was difficult to distinguish from recklessness
A professional whose reputation was built on the risks he took
quiz.answer: A recklessly daring person — one who takes extreme risks
quiz.explanation:
A daredevil is a reckless risk-taker — someone who dares the devil.
• daredevil = a recklessly daring person
• From 'dare' + 'devil' — one who dares as if the devil himself
• 'Daredevil stunt', 'a complete daredevil' — recklessly daring

quiz.question: The blacksmith shaped the hot metal on the anvil. What is an anvil?
options: A heavy iron block on which metal is hammered into shape
The central tool in a blacksmith's workshop
A metal surface designed to absorb the force of hammer blows
The block that supported the metal while it was being worked
quiz.answer: A heavy iron block on which metal is hammered into shape
quiz.explanation:
An anvil is a heavy iron block — used in smithing to shape hot metal.
• anvil = a heavy iron block on which metal is worked
• From Old English: 'onfilti' — on-felt
• 'Blacksmith's anvil', 'on the anvil' — in the process of being shaped

quiz.question: The committee voted to blackball the candidate, preventing his membership. What does 'blackball' mean?
options: To vote against and exclude — to bar from membership
To use an anonymous vote to prevent someone from joining
To exercise a veto in a membership selection process
To decline to support a candidate whose admission was proposed
quiz.answer: To vote against and exclude — to bar from membership
quiz.explanation:
To blackball means to vote against — to exclude from membership by voting.
• blackball = to reject by secret ballot; to exclude
• From placing a black ball in the ballot box to signal rejection
• 'Blackballed from the club' — voted out by secret ballot

quiz.question: The contract contained several hidden pitfalls that could prove costly. What is a pitfall?
options: A hidden danger or unseen difficulty
A clause that would create problems only if a certain event occurred
A risk that was not apparent from a surface reading of the document
A problem that appeared only once the contract was being executed
quiz.answer: A hidden danger or unseen difficulty
quiz.explanation:
A pitfall is a hidden danger — a trap or unseen difficulty.
• pitfall = a hidden danger or unseen difficulty
• From 'pit' + 'fall' — an animal trap hidden in a pit
• 'Avoid the pitfalls', 'hidden pitfalls' — unexpected dangers

quiz.question: News of the accident cast a pall over the entire celebration. What does 'cast a pall' mean?
options: To spread a feeling of gloom — to darken the mood
To introduce information that made everyone reconsider their feelings
To produce sadness in people who had been in good spirits
To transform an occasion through the intrusion of bad news
quiz.answer: To spread a feeling of gloom — to darken the mood
quiz.explanation:
To cast a pall means to spread gloom — to darken the atmosphere.
• pall = a cloth draped over a coffin; an atmosphere of gloom
• From Latin: 'pallium' — a cloak
• 'Cast a pall over', 'a pall of smoke' — a dark covering

quiz.question: She was in thrall to her ambition in a way that crowded out everything else. What does 'in thrall to' mean?
options: Completely captivated and controlled by — enslaved to
So driven by a single goal that other priorities could not compete
Committed to a purpose in a way that felt like a form of compulsion
Pursuing something with a dedication others found difficult to understand
quiz.answer: Completely captivated and controlled by — enslaved to
quiz.explanation:
In thrall to means completely captivated — under the power of.
• thrall = a state of complete subjection or captivity
• From Old Norse: 'thrall' — a slave
• 'In thrall to money', 'held in thrall' — captivated to the point of enslavement
